content.assessment: Networked retention aligns well with an active political narrative — the Magyar Falu Program's rural-revival framing and funding stream — offering a plausible coalition (central government village-development apparatus plus municipalities) willing to sustain a non-closure model. However, its cooperative premise runs against documented inter-municipal competitive dynamics and depends on social capital shown in only one clearly documented case, raising doubts about whether the coalition can be replicated at scale rather than remaining a boutique success.
opportunities: Severe depopulation in a large number of localities (147 facing eventual depopulation, 645 under 300 residents, 114 with under-18 shares below 10%) creates a policy vacuum that a hub/network model, backed by the Magyar Falu Program's rural narrative, could plausibly fill politically (F-live-political_feasibility-04, F-live-political_feasibility-05)
strengths: Directly compatible with an active governing narrative and funding vehicle (Magyar Falu Program: 600 localities, EUR 28.3 million), giving it access to an existing political coalition and legitimacy frame (F-live-political_feasibility-04)

Avoids the acute political flashpoint of closure, likely reducing the probability of the kind of reversal-triggering mobilization seen elsewhere (F-live-political_feasibility-07, F-live-political_feasibility-08)
threats: Documented budget dysfunction in the centralized system (chronic KLIK deficits) raises doubt about the fiscal sustainability of a resource-intensive networked model layered atop existing centralized financing (F-live-political_feasibility-06)

Long historical record of abrupt regime-driven reversals in rural school-network structure suggests any new networked model, however popular initially, carries non-trivial reversal risk under a future political shift (F-live-political_feasibility-09)
weaknesses: The clearest evidence for successful local-network cooperation (Erdőkürt-type case) is a single documented instance contingent on rare social capital, casting doubt on the scalability of a cooperative coalition model (F-live-political_feasibility-03)
No dedicated statutory basis currently exists for a networked model, creating an implementation gap that itself invites administrative and political contestation
